title: Mirage
description: Mirage is a privacy layer for stablecoin transfers on Ethereum L1 that makes private transactions indistinguishable from ordinary onchain activity. It avoids shared pools by using per-transaction escrow contracts whose EVM bytecode is obfuscated by Azoth, an open-source Rust obfuscator that produces many deterministic variants of the same contract logic. Execution is carried out by a decentralised set of Nomad operator nodes that post bonds into the escrow and earn rewards for settling transfers. The protocol is currently in closed alpha with a public test environment in preparation.

properties.name: Confidentiality
properties.notes: Amounts and balances are public, so the answer is No. Alice's wallet deposits the payout (100 USDC) plus a 2 USDC tip into the per-transaction escrow , and the node then sends 100 USDC from its own wallet directly to Bob, so on-chain it looks like a random address transferring 100 USDC . Transfer amounts and per-address balances of the underlying stablecoin are therefore visible on-chain in plaintext; the privacy property provided is unlinkability between sender and recipient, not confidentiality of amounts.

citations.cited_text:
Alice’s wallet deposits into this escrow:

100 USDC (the amount she wants Bob to receive)

2 USDC (a small tip to reward whoever completes the transfer)

At the same time, the Mirage app encrypts Alice’s instructions: “Send 100 USDC to Bob’s address.

If they want to take the job, here's what happens:

The node posts a security deposit into Alice's escrow contract (ensuring they only profit by completing the job honestly)

The node sends 100 USDC from their own wallet directly to Bob via another address 

This is the key moment. Onchain, it looks like this node (some random address) is sending Bob 100 USDC.

properties.name: Anonymity
properties.notes: Mirage provides unlinkability rather than hidden endpoints: both the sender's escrow deployment and the recipient's incoming transfer are visible on-chain, but nothing obvious ties them together. Instead of a direct sender-to-recipient transaction, the app sets up a mechanism where a third party sends the funds on the sender's behalf, so to a blockchain observer it appears that a random address sent the recipient the amount. A node posts a security deposit into the escrow and then sends the funds from its own wallet to the recipient via another address; on-chain this looks like a random address paying the recipient, with no visible connection back to the sender. An observer sees the sender deploying a contract and depositing funds, a separate random address later paying the recipient, and another address withdrawing from the contract — three events with no visible connection between sender and recipient. The sender's funding transaction and the recipient's receipt are both publicly observable as individual events, so neither party is hidden in isolation; the privacy property achieved is the breaking of the linkage between them via the intermediary escrow plus node settlement.

citations.cited_text: Instead of Alice sending directly to Bob (which would be visible onchain and link them together), the Mirage app sets up a mechanism where someone else sends the money to Bob on Alice’s behalf. To anyone watching the blockchain, it just looks like a random address sent Bob 100 USDC.

If they want to take the job, here's what happens:

The node posts a security deposit into Alice's escrow contract (ensuring they only profit by completing the job honestly)

The node sends 100 USDC from their own wallet directly to Bob via another address 

This is the key moment. Onchain, it looks like this node (some random address) is sending Bob 100 USDC. There's no visible connection to Alice.

Why This Provides Privacy 

Let's step back and look at what an observer sees when analyzing the blockchain:

Transaction 1: Alice deployed a contract and deposited some USDC into it
Interpretation: Could be anything. A multisig, an MEV contract, an automated trading bot, whatever 

Transaction 2: Some random address sent Bob 100 USDC
Interpretation: Just a normal transfer. Happens thousands of times per day 

Transaction 3: Another random address later withdrew funds from Alice's contract
Interpretation: who knows? 

There's no visible connection between Alice and Bob.

properties.name: Asset privacy
properties.notes: No. Mirage is a stablecoin transfer protocol where the asset being moved is directly observable on-chain — the blockchain activity looks like an ordinary stablecoin transfer, with observers able to see a deposit into a temporary contract, a standard transfer from another address to the recipient, and a later withdrawal from the escrow contract . Nodes provide their own stablecoin liquidity (e.g. USDC) to execute transfers , and the token identity is visible at both escrow deposit and recipient payout with no commitment scheme concealing which asset moves. Privacy is scoped to unlinking sender and recipient rather than hiding asset identity.

citations.cited_text:
Mirage is designed so that the blockchain activity looks like an ordinary stablecoin transfer.

Observers may see:

A deposit into a temporary contract

A standard transfer from another address to the recipient

A later withdrawal from the escrow contract

Because each transaction uses a unique contract and does not interact with a recognizable privacy pool, observers cannot determine that a privacy protocol was used without spending significant resources on identifying a specific target.
Mirage transactions rely on nodes that provide their own liquidity to execute transfers. Nodes must maintain a minimum amount of stablecoin liquidity (e.g. USDC) in order to execute user requests.

properties.name: Plausible deniability
properties.notes: Entry into the protocol is designed to be indistinguishable from an ordinary on-chain transfer. A stated core idea is that no observer should know a privacy protocol was used, positioning this as a design goal differentiating it from existing privacy solutions which are easy to flag when users interact with CEXs or on/off-ramps. In practice, per-transaction escrow bytecode is rewritten into deterministic variants so the deposit resembles a routine transfer to an unverified contract, and the recipient sees a USDC transfer from a fresh EOA, leaving no "Mirage"-tagged footprint on either side. Note: deniability applies to the deposit/entry path; the settlement leg performed by the Nomad network is the visible protocol-side interaction and is not covered by sender-side deniability. The protocol is in closed alpha as of April 2026, so this assessment reflects the documented design rather than mainnet behaviour.

Menu
Chevron Down 

Menu 

The Undetectability Challenge

On this page
Chevron Right 

The Undetectability Challenge 

One of the core ideas behind Mirage is that no one should know you used a privacy protocol to begin with .

Mirage is the first privacy solution that makes an attempt at protecting its users against the high-risk stigma of using privacy protocols.

properties.name: Time-to-finality
properties.notes: Time-to-finality is N/A because Mirage is an application-layer protocol that settles on host chains and inherits their finality. End-to-end processing times quoted are under ~90 seconds on Ethereum mainnet, under ~6 seconds on Layer 2 networks such as Base or Arbitrum, and under ~4 seconds on the Tempo testnet, though actual processing time depends on network block times and congestion. These represent node settlement latency rather than cryptographic finality, which is determined by the underlying chain's consensus.

citations.cited_text:
The following time indications represent the time for the network of nodes to process and settle the transaction:

Ethereum mainnet: less than ~90 seconds

Layer 2 networks (e.g. Base or Arbitrum): under ~6 seconds

Tempo (Testnet): under ~4 seconds

Actual processing time depends on network block times and congestion.

properties.name: Number of secrets
properties.notes: One secret — the sender's ordinary Ethereum wallet key (secp256k1 ECDSA). The protocol derives no keys of its own and adds no viewing key, spending key, nullifier seed, or note-decryption key; the sender signs the escrow deployment and funding directly with their existing wallet, and the recipient simply receives funds at their normal address with no Mirage-specific signing. The wallet signature is the one secret in use because the user must hold it to authorise any transfer.

citations.cited_text: Instead of Alice sending directly to Bob (which would be visible onchain and link them together), the Mirage app sets up a mechanism where someone else sends the money to Bob on Alice’s behalf. To anyone watching the blockchain, it just looks like a random address sent Bob 100 USDC.

Step 1: Setting Up the Escrow 

When Alice hits send, she deploys a small, temporary escrow contract through the Mirage app. This contract handles the transaction safely and privately.

properties.name: Deposit time
properties.notes: Deposit time is 0 seconds — there is no mandatory waiting period before funding a Mirage transfer. When the sender initiates a transfer, they deploy a small, temporary escrow contract that handles the transaction safely and privately. The sender's wallet deposits the transfer amount and tip into this escrow in the same flow , so deployment and deposit occur together in a single user-initiated action with no enforced delay.

citations.cited_text:
Step 1: Setting Up the Escrow 

When Alice hits send, she deploys a small, temporary escrow contract through the Mirage app. This contract handles the transaction safely and privately.

Alice’s wallet deposits into this escrow:

100 USDC (the amount she wants Bob to receive)

2 USDC (a small tip to reward whoever completes the transfer)

At the same time, the Mirage app encrypts Alice’s instructions: “Send 100 USDC to Bob’s address.

properties.name: Withdraw time
properties.notes: There is no protocol-enforced withdraw delay. The sender can reclaim funds from the escrow at any time without permission, provided a node has not already bonded against it; once a node bonds, withdrawal is paused until the node either completes settlement or the 5-minute bond deadline expires, after which the sender can withdraw. Recipient-side settlement through the Nomad network takes ~90 seconds on Ethereum and ~6 seconds on L2s, but that is node-settlement latency rather than a protocol-imposed withdraw time.

citations.cited_text:
The following time indications represent the time for the network of nodes to process and settle the transaction:

Ethereum mainnet: less than ~90 seconds

Layer 2 networks (e.g. Base or Arbitrum): under ~6 seconds

Tempo (Testnet): under ~4 seconds

Actual processing time depends on network block times and congestion.
The user can withdraw funds from the smart contract at any time without needing any permissions, as long as a node has not already submitted a security deposit to finish the transaction.

properties.name: Censorship resistance
properties.notes: No. The executor set is permissioned and subject to platform-level revocation: nodes run a lightweight enclave that manages funds and executes signals, and in the event of a vulnerability the platform issues a signature revoking the enclave, blocking clients from producing any more signals for nodes . Because signals propagate through a libp2p gossip layer where nodes randomly sample them for execution , a user whose signal is ignored by every Nomad has no fallback path to force inclusion and must cancel and withdraw from the escrow. Node admission is gated by the team (operators must contact Mirage to be onboarded), so the entity set that can execute transfers is closed and centrally controllable.

citations.cited_text:
Mitigating Vulnerabilities 

Nodes run a lightweight enclave that manages the funds and handles executing signals.
In the event of a vulnerability or hack, the platform will issue a signature revoking the enclave, blocking any clients from producing any more signals for nodes.
The signal propagates through a libp2p gossip layer, where nodes receive incoming signals and randomly sample them for execution.

properties.name: External network dependence
properties.notes: Mirage relies on the external Nomad network, which is a permissioned set of operators. The Nomad network consists of TEE-backed nodes that decrypt, validate, and execute client signals , meaning every private transfer depends on these external actors to process the encrypted signal. Node operation requires a dedicated SGX machine and at least $10k in USDC liquidity , and onboarding is handled directly by the Mirage team rather than open registration. The platform retains the ability to issue a signature revoking the enclave and later sign and distribute a new enclave to nodes , confirming central gatekeeping over which operators can participate.

citations.cited_text:
Menu
Chevron Down 

Menu 

Nomad Overview

On this page
Chevron Right 

Nomad Overview 

The Nomad network is a network of TEE-backed nodes that decrypt, validate, and execute client signals without exposing transaction details or node activity to any specific party.

Inside each node's SGX enclave, the signal is decrypted and processed: 

The enclave validates the escrow contract bytecode

Bonds tokens using one of its internal EOAs

Executes the transfer from another EOA

Builds a Merkle proof for the transfer and collects the reward by submitting the proof to the escrow contract using the bonded EOA

Basic requirements 

To run a node, you must be able to provide the following requirements:

At least $10k in USDC liquidity

Dedicated SGX machine (cloud-hosted)

Interested in joining the network and running your own node?

In the event of a vulnerability or hack, the platform will issue a signature revoking the enclave, blocking any clients from producing any more signals for nodes.
Once a sufficient mitigation is released by the platform, a new enclave is signed and distributed to nodes to resume work.

properties.name: Escape hatch
properties.value: Can exit in a time period
properties.notes: Users can withdraw funds from the escrow without any external approval: the sender can withdraw funds from the smart contract at any time without needing any permissions, as long as a node has not already submitted a security deposit to finish the transaction. Once a node has bonded, withdrawal is paused until either the node completes execution or the 5-minute bond deadline expires, after which the sender can reclaim the funds. Because each transfer uses a transaction-specific escrow rather than a shared pool, exit depends only on the host chain's consensus enforcing the escrow contract logic.

citations.cited_text: The user can withdraw funds from the smart contract at any time without needing any permissions, as long as a node has not already submitted a security deposit to finish the transaction.

properties.name: Upgradeability
properties.notes: Upgrade authority for Mirage sits with the central team (Single admin), because although each deployed escrow instance is immutable, the team controls the toolchain and operator set that define protocol behaviour across future transfers. The current escrow is published as a testing implementation with simplified mechanics, and the production contract is stated to differ in implementation details while maintaining the core bonding logic. The Azoth bytecode obfuscator, the enclave signing key for Nomad operators, and the forthcoming production escrow are all controlled by the Mirage team, giving a single party the ability to ship a successor contract and revoke executors that users must migrate to. Per-instance parameters are fixed at deployment — the contract ensures security through immutable deployment parameters, time-based execution deadlines, and bond forfeiture mechanisms — but this instance-level immutability does not constrain protocol-level upgrades.

citations.cited_text: **Note**: This is a testing implementation with simplified mechanics. The production contract will differ in implementation details while maintaining the core bonding logic.

This single-task escrow operates with ERC20 tokens and manages funds, bonds, and payments through a time-based bonding mechanism:

- **Funding**: Deployers fund the contract with reward and payment amounts for task completion
- **Bonding**: Nomads post bonds (minimum 50% of reward) to secure execution rights within a 5-minute deadline
- **Collection**: Successful Nomads collect their bond plus rewards and payments, fully draining the contract
- **Forfeiture**: Failed Nomads forfeit their bonds, which are added to the reward pool for subsequent attempts
- **Withdrawal**: Deployers can withdraw original funds when no active bonds exist or request cancellation to prevent new bonds

The contract ensures security through immutable deployment parameters, time-based execution deadlines, and bond forfeiture mechanisms that incentivize reliable task completion by Nomads.

properties.name: Client-side proving
properties.notes: No. Mirage transfers rely on TEE-based execution rather than client-generated zero-knowledge proofs: clients encrypt their signal (containing recipient, amount, etc.) using the enclave's global secp256k1 public key and send it to the network , which is an encryption step, not a proving step. The only proof involved is produced server-side by Nomad operators, where the enclave builds a Merkle proof for the transfer and collects the reward by submitting the proof to the escrow contract using the bonded EOA . Privacy derives from Azoth-obfuscated per-transaction escrows and SGX-backed Nomad execution, so no client-device proving system is in use.

citations.cited_text: Clients encrypt their signal (containing recipient, amount, ...) using the enclave's global secp256k1 public key, and send it to the network.

Inside each node's SGX enclave, the signal is decrypted and processed: 

The enclave validates the escrow contract bytecode

Bonds tokens using one of its internal EOAs

Executes the transfer from another EOA

Builds a Merkle proof for the transfer and collects the reward by submitting the proof to the escrow contract using the bonded EOA

Basic requirements 

To run a node, you must be able to provide the following requirements:

At least $10k in USDC liquidity

Dedicated SGX machine (cloud-hosted)

Interested in joining the network and running your own node?

properties.name: Third-party inspectability
properties.notes: Yes. Inside each Nomad node's SGX enclave, the encrypted signal is decrypted and processed, with the enclave validating the escrow bytecode, bonding tokens from an internal EOA, executing the transfer from another EOA, and building a Merkle proof to claim the reward , which means the recipient, amount, and token are exposed in cleartext inside a third-party operator's machine. Trust rests on SGX attestation rather than on the operator, so a node operator who compromises their enclave host can inspect sender, recipient, amount, and token for any signal their node samples. Signals propagate through a libp2p gossip layer where nodes randomly sample them for execution , distributing this inspection capability across the operator set rather than eliminating it.

properties.name: Implementation maturity
properties.notes: Mirage is not yet deployed on mainnet; as of April 2026 it is in closed alpha with a public testnet (Tempo) and beta release in preparation. Wallet screening and compliance tooling are described as arriving in the upcoming Beta release , and settlement timings are benchmarked against Tempo as a testnet environment alongside illustrative figures for Ethereum and L2s. The escrow component is a testing implementation with the production contract still pending. Needs verification: if Tempo is publicly accessible, the correct tier is "2 : Public testnet" rather than "1 : Prototype / devnet".

citations.cited_text: As of the upcoming Beta release, when connecting a wallet to the Mirage web application, the system will perform wallet screening, risk scoring, and analysis of the origin of funds through a compliance partner integration, allowing Mirage to ensure compliance with Anti-Money Laundering (AML) and Countering the Financing of Terrorism (CFT).

properties.name: Post-quantum secure
properties.notes: Mirage is not post-quantum secure. User authentication depends on Ethereum-native secp256k1 ECDSA signatures, which are broken by Shor's algorithm on a sufficiently capable quantum computer. The off-chain coordination layer relies on encrypted signals broadcast to executors, locked behind a cryptographic puzzle that must be solved before contents can be read , using ephemeral asymmetric encryption that is likewise vulnerable to quantum attack, alongside Intel SGX attestation for Nomad operators — none of these primitives offer post-quantum guarantees.

citations.cited_text: When you want to make a private transaction, your wallet broadcasts an encrypted message (called a 'signal') to a network of independent executors. Each signal is locked behind a cryptographic puzzle that must be solved before its contents can be read.

properties.name: Layer of enforcement
properties.notes: No compliance enforcement is active on the canonical protocol in its current closed-alpha state. Wallet screening, risk scoring, and origin-of-funds analysis through a compliance-partner integration are documented as arriving with the upcoming Beta release, but are not yet live; no blockchain-level or asset-level hook exists either, since the escrow contracts only reimburse nodes after cryptographic verification of execution and do not embed allowlists or blocklists.

citations.cited_text: As of the upcoming Beta release, when connecting a wallet to the Mirage web application, the system will perform wallet screening, risk scoring, and analysis of the origin of funds through a compliance partner integration, allowing Mirage to ensure compliance with Anti-Money Laundering (AML) and Countering the Financing of Terrorism (CFT). This allows Mirage to gatekeep its use from illicit users.

properties.name: Enforcement entities
properties.notes: No entity currently enforces a compliance policy on Mirage transfers. The Mirage team signs the Nomad enclave binaries and can revoke them in the event of a vulnerability, but that is a security-operations lever over the executor network, not a user-level compliance gate. When Beta-stage wallet screening ships, the Mirage team becomes the admin-level enforcer — today there is no such role.

citations.cited_text:
Mitigating Vulnerabilities 

Nodes run a lightweight enclave that manages the funds and handles executing signals.
In the event of a vulnerability or hack, the platform will issue a signature revoking the enclave, blocking any clients from producing any more signals for nodes.
Once a sufficient mitigation is released by the platform, a new enclave is signed and distributed to nodes to resume work.

properties.name: Type of compliance
properties.notes: The canonical protocol at closed-alpha stage implements no compliance mechanism. A KYT-style wallet-screening check is documented for the upcoming Beta but is not yet active. Voluntary disclosure is a design philosophy — the sender can always publish the escrow address they deployed — rather than a protocol-defined selective-disclosure primitive (no viewing key, no scoped delegation). The design avoids pooled funds, so no POI/ASP-style blocklist proofs apply.

citations.cited_text: You should not be associated with criminals and be able to reveal the transaction path of your funds at your discretion.

Not bring regulatory liability: Your funds shouldn't touch others the way they do in a mixer or pool-based design. You should not be associated with criminals and be able to reveal the transaction path of your funds at your discretion.

properties.name: Point of enforcement
properties.notes: No compliance gate is active at deposit, transfer, or withdrawal. Each escrow is a one-shot contract with no in-protocol transfer step and a user-controlled cancel path at exit. Planned Beta wallet screening will gate at deposit (signal acceptance) once shipped; until then there is nothing to enforce.

citations.cited_text: As of the upcoming Beta release, when connecting a wallet to the Mirage web application, the system will perform wallet screening, risk scoring, and analysis of the origin of funds through a compliance partner integration, allowing Mirage to ensure compliance with Anti-Money Laundering (AML) and Countering the Financing of Terrorism (CFT). This allows Mirage to gatekeep its use from illicit users.

properties.name: Selective disclosure: viewing entity
properties.notes: There is no viewing-key primitive in Mirage. The sender can always re-identify their own flow by publishing the escrow address they deployed and the payout transaction it produced, but that is ordinary public-chain visibility rather than a protocol-defined disclosure channel granted to a third party. No involuntary channel or protocol-level viewer exists either.

citations.cited_text: You should not be associated with criminals and be able to reveal the transaction path of your funds at your discretion.
Transfers appear indistinguishable from ordinary transactions while remaining fully auditable when necessary.

properties.name: Selective disclosure: viewing control
properties.notes: No viewing-key system exists, so there is nothing to scope, revoke, or time-limit. Viewing entity is None; viewing control follows.

citations.cited_text:
The Plan 

Mirage delivers flexible, undetectable privacy for stablecoin transactions on Ethereum L1. Flexible means users define privacy guarantees, acceptable costs, and compliance requirements. Transfers appear indistinguishable from ordinary transactions while remaining fully auditable when necessary.

properties.name: Cryptographic verifiability
properties.notes: Correctness of settlement relies on a mix of cryptographic proofs and hardware attestation, so the answer is No (not purely cryptographic). The enclave validates the escrow bytecode, bonds tokens, executes the transfer, and then builds a Merkle proof for the transfer which it submits to the escrow contract to collect the reward , giving the on-chain payout leg a cryptographic check. However, the Nomad execution path depends on a lightweight SGX enclave that manages funds and handles signals, with the platform able to revoke the enclave via signature in the event of a vulnerability and later sign and distribute a new enclave , meaning trust ultimately rests on Intel SGX attestation and the Mirage team's signing authority rather than consensus-level cryptographic verification. Protocol status is closed alpha, so no mainnet correctness guarantees apply yet.

Bonds tokens using one of its internal EOAs

Basic requirements

Interested in joining the network and running your own node?

Mitigating Vulnerabilities 

Nodes run a lightweight enclave that manages the funds and handles executing signals.
In the event of a vulnerability or hack, the platform will issue a signature revoking the enclave, blocking any clients from producing any more signals for nodes.
Once a sufficient mitigation is released by the platform, a new enclave is signed and distributed to nodes to resume work.

properties.name: Open source
properties.notes: No — not every critical component of Mirage carries an OSI-approved open source licence. Azoth, the deterministic EVM bytecode obfuscator written in Rust, is published under the MIT licence , but the escrow contract used to facilitate compensation for Nomad executor nodes ships without a LICENSE file and is marked as a testing implementation rather than the production contract. The Nomad executor/enclave code, which handles node-side key operations, is not published in the MiragePrivacy organisation at all, leaving a core component of the system closed.

properties.name: Private State Scalability
properties.notes: Mirage's private state is **Stateless** — the protocol does not maintain a growing Merkle tree, note set, or nullifier registry. Each transfer deploys a unique per-transaction smart contract that, to outside observers, looks like a normal unverified contract of the kind appearing on Ethereum every day. These escrow contracts are funded and drained per-transfer and then become inert, so cumulative protocol-specific private state does not accumulate with usage. Coordination happens off-chain via encrypted signals broadcast to a network of independent executors , meaning there is no shared on-chain anonymity set whose size grows over time.

citations.cited_text:
Self-Sovereignty and Compliance 

Instead of using obvious mixer contracts, Mirage creates different unique smart contracts for each transaction. To outside observers, these look like normal, unverified contracts that appear on Ethereum every day.

Mirage solves this by moving all coordination off-chain. When you want to make a private transaction, your wallet broadcasts an encrypted message (called a 'signal') to a network of independent executors.

properties.name: Client-side indexing
properties.notes: No scanning is required on the client side. Recipients receive funds through a direct on-chain USDC transfer from a node's wallet, which appears as a regular address sending them stablecoins , so their wallet picks it up as any ordinary incoming transfer without protocol-specific scanning. Senders deploy a transaction-specific escrow contract when initiating the transfer , meaning they only need to track their own escrow rather than sweep the chain for commitments or notes. There is no shielded pool, nullifier set, or note commitment tree that would require continuous chain scanning to reconstruct balances.

citations.cited_text:
If they want to take the job, here's what happens:

The node posts a security deposit into Alice's escrow contract (ensuring they only profit by completing the job honestly)

The node sends 100 USDC from their own wallet directly to Bob via another address 

This is the key moment. Onchain, it looks like this node (some random address) is sending Bob 100 USDC.

Step 1: Setting Up the Escrow 

When Alice hits send, she deploys a small, temporary escrow contract through the Mirage app.

properties.name: Private state model
properties.notes: Mirage's model is closest to an account-based one, though the property does not really apply — the protocol maintains no private shielded state of its own. Each transfer deploys a small, temporary escrow contract that handles the transaction , and the node sends the payment from their own wallet directly to the recipient via another address , meaning balances remain as ordinary ERC-20 balances on the host chain rather than as UTXO commitments or a shared private pool. There are no nullifiers, notes, or pooled private state — privacy derives from unlinkability of the escrow and the executing node, not from a private accounting layer.

citations.cited_text:
Step 1: Setting Up the Escrow 

When Alice hits send, she deploys a small, temporary escrow contract through the Mirage app. This contract handles the transaction safely and privately.

If they want to take the job, here's what happens:

The node posts a security deposit into Alice's escrow contract (ensuring they only profit by completing the job honestly)

The node sends 100 USDC from their own wallet directly to Bob via another address 

This is the key moment.

properties.name: Private Data Storage
properties.notes: Mirage's design has no persistent private state stored on-chain — the closest fit is off-chain storage, though the property does not cleanly apply to an ephemeral-contract model. Clients encrypt their signal (containing recipient, amount, and other fields) using the enclave's global secp256k1 public key and send it to the network, where it propagates through a libp2p gossip layer and nodes randomly sample signals for execution. Inside each node's SGX enclave the signal is decrypted, the escrow contract bytecode is validated, tokens are bonded from an internal EOA, the transfer is executed from another EOA, and a Merkle proof is submitted to the escrow contract to collect the reward. No encrypted transaction data resides in a long-lived contract; the only on-chain artefacts are the disposable escrow and the payout itself, while the encrypted signal lives transiently in the off-chain gossip layer and inside enclave memory.

The signal propagates through a libp2p gossip layer, where nodes receive incoming signals and randomly sample them for execution.

Inside each node's SGX enclave, the signal is decrypted and processed:

Bonds tokens using one of its internal EOAs

Executes the transfer from another EOA

Builds a Merkle proof for the transfer and collects the reward by submitting the proof to the escrow contract using the bonded EOA

Basic requirements 

To run a node, you must be able to provide the following requirements:

At least $10k in USDC liquidity

Dedicated SGX machine (cloud-hosted)

Interested in joining the network and running your own node?

properties.name: Access to DeFi
properties.notes: Mirage offers unlimited access to DeFi applications on the destination chain, since the protocol terminates each private transfer by delivering plaintext USDC to an ordinary recipient wallet, from which any DeFi protocol can be used without restriction. The protocol itself does not operate a shielded DeFi ecosystem or expose composable hooks while funds are in transit; the on-chain footprint consists of a deposit into what appears to be an ordinary contract and a standard transfer to the intended recipient . Because the output is a normal token transfer to a regular address, the recipient inherits the full DeFi surface of the destination chain (Ethereum or supported L2s) with no protocol-imposed whitelist. Note: as of April 2026 the protocol is in closed alpha, so this behaviour is not yet live on mainnet.

citations.cited_text: When you use Mirage, your on-chain footprint consists entirely of routine-looking interactions: depositing tokens into what appears to be an ordinary contract, and someone else later making a standard transfer to your intended recipient.

properties.name: Programmability / Generality
properties.notes: Mirage's programmability is limited to payment flows, specifically private stablecoin transfers executed through a one-shot escrow pattern. Each transaction deploys a unique smart contract that to outside observers looks like a normal, unverified contract , rather than exposing a general-purpose private execution environment or shielded DeFi primitives. The on-chain footprint consists of depositing tokens into what appears to be an ordinary contract, and someone else later making a standard transfer to the intended recipient — a fixed deposit-and-forward flow with no private state persistence or composability with other protocols.

citations.cited_text:
Self-Sovereignty and Compliance 

Instead of using obvious mixer contracts, Mirage creates different unique smart contracts for each transaction. To outside observers, these look like normal, unverified contracts that appear on Ethereum every day.

When you use Mirage, your on-chain footprint consists entirely of routine-looking interactions: depositing tokens into what appears to be an ordinary contract, and someone else later making a standard transfer to your intended recipient.
